para:
After the SFA refused to take part in World Cup 1950 in Brazil due to pride after finishing second in qualifying instead of first, they were met with pleas from players and fans alike. They decided to correct this and grant the nation the right to attend World Cup 1954 in Switzerland despite again finishing second in qualifying. 

Unfortunately, the tournament is remembered by Scotland as one of poor preparation. While being allowed 18 player slots, the SFA selected only 13 to play at the tournament and, as a result, were stretched thin in squad depth. However, the worries didn't stop there, as the team had also brought heavy woollen kits that were unsuitable for the Swiss summer conditions, and additionally had then manager, Andy Beattie, resign after just the very first match, where his side lost 1–0 to Austria at the Hardturm Stadium in Zurich after disagreement with the SFA over such poor organisation.

The second and final match of the tournament was an admittedly demoralised Scotland set to face World Cup defending champions Uruguay at the St. Jakob Stadium in Basel, where Uruguay ran away as 7–0 victors, the worst international defeat in Scottish footballing history. This left Scotland rock bottom of the group with 0 points and 0 goals.
fact:
Scotland player Tommy Docherty made reference to the SFA's blunder regarding kits and the Swiss climate, stating
"by the time the anthems were played, we were knackered".

para:
Seeking to learn from the past, Scotland properly utilised the full 22-man squad limit and were set for the 1958 tournament in Sweden with the legendary Sir Matt Busby set to be at the helm. Tragically, four months before the competition, Busby was severely injured in the Munich air disaster, which claimed the lives of 23 people. Trainer Dawson Walker stepped in to lead the national side as a result.

The team went on to grab their first ever World Cup point in a 1–1 draw against Yugoslavia thanks to a Jimmy Murray equaliser in the 50th minute at the Arosvallen stadium. Scotland then went on to frustratingly lose 3–2 against Paraguay in their second group match at the Idrottsparken. This led to a dramatic last game in Örebro where a result was needed against France. Scotland fought valiantly but ultimately lost 2–1 due to a John Hewie penalty miss that hit the post, a moment that could have changed Scottish football history.
fact: This was also the only year all four Home Nations successfully qualified for the World Cup.

para:
Scotland had to wait 16 years before another successful qualification campaign and they aimed to be back with a bang as their squad featured several legends of the game including Kenny Dalglish, Dennis Law, Danny McGrain, Jimmy Johnstone, and captain Billy Bremner.

The tournament began with Scotland picking up their first long-awaited win at a World Cup against Zaire, where they won 2–0 in Dortmund at the Westfalenstadion. Scotland's next match-ups were both to be played in Frankfurt. The first was defending champions Brazil, where Scotland heroically held on for a respectable 0–0, including an infamous close-by chance that Bremner dragged wide. The final game of the Group saw Scotland face Yugoslavia, with both teams on 3 points and Brazil leading against Zaire. The winner would be guaranteed qualification into the knockout rounds. The match concluded with a scoreline of 1–1 with both teams scoring in the last 10 minutes. This left Scotland, Yugoslavia, and Brazil all on 4 points with Zaire on 0.

Due to both Brazil and Yugoslavia scoring more goals against Zaire, they qualified ahead of Scotland on goal difference, causing Scottish heartbreak and making them the first nation in history to be eliminated from a World Cup without losing a game.
fact: Jimmy Johnstone was so highly rated that Brazil’s manager spent weeks planning how to stop him. However, due to a few too many legendary nights out between warm-up games, Johnstone had to spend the entire tournament on the bench!

para:
After the solid performance in the last tournament, the team headed to Argentina with unprecedented expectations. Manager Ally MacLeod famously claimed his side would return with "a medal of some sort".

Despite the high optimism, the opening games in Córdoba were a disaster. Scotland underestimated Peru and suffered a convincing 1–3 defeat. They then went on to play World Cup debutants Iran, where MacLeod's men again underperformed, and it finished 1–1. This is often cited as one of the more embarrassing results in the nation's history. Due to the rough start, Scotland needed to win by at least 3 against the Netherlands in order to qualify for the next round. Although failing to do so, Scotland still put on a brilliant performance in Mendoza against the eventual finalist. Scotland won 3–2 with a Kenny Dalglish goal and Archie Gemmill scoring twice, one of which being one of the most iconic World Cup goals ever scored.
fact: A huge crowd at Hampden Park gave the team an ecstatic send-off, with the team paraded before flying to South America.

para:
Scotland had yet again produced a team full of some of the world's best. The squad featured six players with 10 European Cup winners' medals won between them in the past 5 years with their respective clubs. The mood was again optimistic,this time under manager Jock Stein, moving past the chaos of the previous campaign.

The competition started well with a 5–2 triumphant win against New Zealand at the Estadio La Rosaleda. Scotland again met Brazil in the World Cup group stage, but this time were dispatched of rather easily in Seville, where they suffered a 4–1 loss despite taking an early lead with a top corner strike from defender David Narey. Returning to Málaga for the final group match versus the Soviet Union, Scotland knew only a win would see them progress. It was a close game with Joe Jordan giving Scotland the lead before the Soviets struck twice in the second half with one goal in the 84th minute coming from a defensive mix-up collision involving Alan Hansen and Willie Miller. Graeme Souness equalised almost immediately. However, the Scots ran out of time and the score finished 2–2, resulting in Scotland being eliminated on goal difference for the third tournament in a row.
fact: By scoring against the Soviet Union, Joe Jordan became the first and only Scottish player to score in three different World Cup finals.

para:
Devastatingly, manager Jock Stein suffered a fatal heart attack on the touchline during the final qualifier against Wales in Cardiff. His assistant, Alex Ferguson, took over as caretaker to lead the team through the remaining play-off against Australia and then the tournament in Mexico.

When the group stage was revealed, Scotland found themselves drawn into the "Group of Death" that featured a talented Denmark, West Germany, and Uruguay. Scotland lost the opener against Denmark 0–1, and got beat 2–1 against West Germany in spite of a good Gordon Strachan finish. This left the final match against Uruguay as a must-win. What transpired was what many consider one of the most infamous games in football history. It started off great for Scotland as Uruguayan defender José Batista was sent off after just 56 seconds, the record for fastest red card in a World Cup. However, the 10-man Uruguay spent the next 89 minutes using every cynical trick to preserve a 0–0 draw. The result saw Scotland get eliminated and finishing bottom with just 1 point.
fact: After elimination, several players decided to host a party at their hotel inspired by the 1978 film National Lampoon's Animal House to lift their spirits.

para:
The sun was beginning to set on Scotland’s greatest generation of talent. The world-class icons who had defined the 70s and 80s were gone, leaving a new core of domestic stars to protect a proud legacy of five consecutive World Cup appearances.

Expectations were high for the first match against World Cup debutants Costa Rica. However, Scotland was stunned by a Juan Cayasso goal early in the second half, making the game finish 1–0 with Scotland struggling to break down the team they had reportedly failed to adequately scout. With manager Andy Roxburgh's job on the line, Scotland picked themselves back up to a 1–2 victory against Sweden thanks to goals from Stuart McCall and Mo Johnston. Scotland went into the final game requiring at least a draw against Brazil. They matched the Brazilians for 80 minutes until substitute Müller tapped in a rebound after Jim Leighton parried a shot, sealing Scotland's fate as they finished third on 2 points.
fact: The Scotland squad recorded an official song titled "Say It With Pride," before the 1990 tournament, featuring guest vocals from various Scottish music legends.

para:
After 8 years, Scotland found their way back to football's biggest international competition, where they had the honour of playing the tournament opener against defending champions Brazil at the Stade de France.

Scotland fell behind early after a 5th minute header from a corner but later levelled the scoring through a penalty kick neatly dispatched by John Collins. Scotland held on until the 74th minute when a Cafu shot was parried by Jim Leighton, only to bounce off Tom Boyd’s chest and into his own net to hand Brazil the 2–1 victory. The next game was against Norway, where they found conceding a goal just one minute into the second half. Through a David Weir long ball, Craig Burley managed to lob the keeper and secure a point to keep the qualification hopes alive. For the final group match versus Morocco, a win was needed to have any chance at advancing into the next round. Unfortunately, Scotland were outclassed 3–0 on the night alongside a frustrated Burely being dismissed, leading to a rock-bottom finish on 1 point.
fact: The squad famously arrived in France wearing bespoke kilts, which became a massive hit with the international media.

para:
Scotland have finally ended their longest World Cup drought ever after 28 years of heartache.

The national team, under current manager Steve Clarke, has potentially seen its best crop of players since the golden generation. The squad's heroics have seen them top their qualification group after a dramatic must-win final game against Denmark that required some of the best goals ever scored in Scotland shirts to secure a 4–2 victory, two of which coming in additional time to drag the nation to dreamland in North America.

Leading the line for Scotland will be veteran captain Andy Robertson and red-hot form midfielder Scott McTominay. Where they will face two familiar opponents in Morocco and Brazil, with a new opponent in Haiti who have just ended their own 52-year wait for a world cup.

The matches against Haiti and Morocco are scheduled to take place at the Gillete Stadium in Boston, Massachusetts, with the Brazil game scheduled for the Hard Rock Stadium in Miami, Florida.
fact: The Scottish Government has designated 15 June 2026 as a national bank holiday to celebrate Scotland's return to the world stage.
